title: Compulsive Exercise and the Body Trying to Outrun It
dek: You run—not from the pavement, but from the lava-hot fury simmering beneath your ribs. Compulsive exercise is often mistaken for discipline or health, yet beneath its rhythmic pounding lies a body desperately trying to outpace its own anger. Exploring this through the lens of somatic wisdom and herbal allies reveals how your muscles, your breath, and your nervous system all speak a language of survival—not failure.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body Knows: When Running Isn’t Just Running

It’s 3:14 a.m., and your diaphragm tightens like a clenched fist in the dark. You wake, heart hammering with a familiar restlessness, the smell of sweat and earth still clinging to your skin from yesterday’s run. The masseter muscle in your jaw clenches involuntarily, a silent echo of the anger you were taught to swallow whole. You lace up your shoes, not because you want to, but because if you don’t, the pressure in your psoas will become unbearable. This is not mere habit; it’s biology. Your body is outrunning something fierce inside.

Compulsive exercise is often misunderstood as willpower or vanity. But beneath the glossy Instagram posts and endorphin-fueled highs lies a somatic story of escape. Your trapezius knots tighten by afternoon, your levator scapulae whisper tension into your neck, and your adrenal glands scream exhaustion. This is your body trying to outpace its own anger, a message from your cells that you refuse to hear.

## The Anatomy of Outrunning Anger

The psoas, often called the “muscle of the soul,” is a deep core stabilizer linking your spine to your legs. When anger lodges in your system, this muscle shortens and hardens, creating a chronic state of fight-or-flight. You don’t just move your legs when you run; you carry the weight of your fury in the very fascia that surrounds the psoas. Every pounding step is a desperate attempt to shake loose the knots of indignation and rage that were never meant to be ignored.

Your diaphragm, the primary muscle of breathing, mirrors this tension. When anger is unexpressed, the diaphragm tightens, restricting your breath and feeding the adrenal cascade. This shallow breathing is the body’s way of holding anger hostage, preventing it from reaching your consciousness in a form you can address. The body remembers what you do not: that anger is not a moral failure but a messenger.

## The Exhausted Adrenals and the Cost of Compulsive Movement

Your adrenal glands, perched like tiny sentinels above your kidneys, release cortisol in response to chronic stress—such as unacknowledged anger. Compulsive exercise pushes these glands into overdrive, prolonging their secretion of stress hormones. This cycle leads to adrenal fatigue, a state of profound exhaustion where even the thought of movement feels like a mountain.

Herbal allies like Ashwagandha and Rhodiola can support this weary system. Ashwagandha, an adaptogen, helps modulate cortisol production and soothe the nervous system. Rhodiola enhances your endurance and resilience, not by masking stress but by helping your body adapt to it. Another gentle support is Milky Oats, which nourishes the nervous system and fosters a sense of calm strength. These herbs are not quick fixes but companions in your somatic journey.

## Somatic Teachers on Anger and the Body

Thich Nhat Hanh’s teachings offer a radical reframing: anger is a form of suffering that, when met with mindful presence, reveals profound insight. He invites you to breathe deeply into the tightness in your diaphragm, to acknowledge the heat in your chest without judgment. This presence softens the masseter’s clenched grip and begins to unknot the psoas’s tension. It’s not about suppressing the fury but befriending it.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist focusing on racialized trauma, emphasizes that the body carries what the mind cannot process. He points to the importance of localized body awareness—feeling the heaviness in your trapezius or the ache in your levator scapulae—as a gateway to healing. Your compulsive running is a visceral attempt to move through that trauma, but without somatic attunement, the body remains trapped in a loop.

## The Mechanics of Running From Anger

When you run compulsively, your gait changes subtly but significantly. The propulsion comes not just from the calves and quads but from a contracted psoas pulling your hip forward with urgency. This chronic contraction restricts spinal mobility and compresses the lumbar discs, creating a feedback loop of pain and tension.

Your breath becomes shallow, trapped high in the chest, disconnected from the diaphragm’s full capacity. The smell of your skin—salt and earth, mingled with the faint herbal residue of yesterday’s tincture—speaks of a body in overdrive. The nervous system is wired to keep moving, to avoid the stillness where anger might surface. Yet this movement does not free you; it binds you tighter.

## The Psoas and the Memory of Flight

The psoas muscle is a master storyteller. It remembers the times you wanted to scream but smiled instead, the moments you wanted to fight back but froze. When you run compulsively, you are not just exercising; you are enacting a primal urge to flee from the anger that feels too dangerous to hold. The psoas, tightly coiled, drives your legs forward, a physical manifestation of the emotional flight response.

This chronic contraction affects more than just your gait. It pulls on your lower back, creating a dull, persistent ache that you might try to stretch away, only to find it returns the moment you stop moving. The fascia surrounding the psoas becomes rigid, a protective armor against the vulnerability of feeling. Your body is trying to protect you, but in doing so, it traps the very emotion it seeks to escape.

## The Adrenal Cascade and the Illusion of Energy

The energy you feel during a compulsive run is often an illusion, a chemical high fueled by adrenaline and cortisol. Your adrenal glands are working overtime, pumping out stress hormones to keep you moving. This is not the sustainable energy of a well-rested body; it is the frantic energy of a body in crisis.

When the run ends, the crash is inevitable. The adrenals, depleted from the constant demand, struggle to maintain balance. You might feel a profound exhaustion, a heaviness in your limbs that makes even simple tasks feel monumental. Yet, the compulsion remains. The fear of what might surface in the stillness drives you to lace up your shoes again, perpetuating the cycle of exhaustion and escape.

## The Jaw Remembers: Anger’s Silent Archive

Your jaw tightens long before you lace your running shoes. The masseter, responsible for chewing and clenching, stores memories of anger and betrayal. When you hold your jaw in tension, you’re telling your body to stay alert, to prepare for fight or flight. This tension radiates down your neck, into the levator scapulae, binding your shoulders in a vice.

Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic psychotherapist, teaches that the jaw is a gateway to emotional release. Allowing the jaw to soften can unlock years of repressed fury. This is not about forcing expression but creating safety in your body to feel and release what was held tight.

## Restoring Balance: From Compulsion to Compassion

Compulsive exercise is not a failure of will but a somatic code, a desperate attempt to outrun a message your body needs you to hear. You are not broken; you are carrying a story in your fascia, muscles, and breath.

Pema Chödrön’s invitation to lean into discomfort applies here: let the tightness in your psoas and the ache in your trapezius be your teachers. When you stop running, even for a moment, and breathe into the tension, you create space for healing.

This is not a path of judgment or shame but of radical acceptance and somatic listening. Your body knows the way; your herbs, breath, and practices are signposts on the road back to yourself.

## Herbal Allies for the Exhausted Body

When the body has been running from its own anger for years, the nervous system becomes frayed, the adrenals depleted, and the fascia tight and brittle. This is where the profound medicine of herbs comes in. You don’t need a quick fix; you need deep, restorative nourishment.

Ashwagandha is a root that speaks directly to the exhausted adrenals. It is an adaptogen, meaning it helps the body adapt to stress rather than simply masking it. When you take Ashwagandha, you are offering your nervous system a grounding anchor. It helps to modulate the cortisol that has been flooding your system, gently coaxing the body out of its chronic fight-or-flight state. It’s not about suppressing the energy, but about grounding it, allowing the frantic pacing to slow into a steady, rooted presence.

Rhodiola, another powerful adaptogen, works differently. It is slightly stimulating but in a way that builds resilience rather than depleting it. If Ashwagandha is the anchor, Rhodiola is the steady wind in your sails. It helps the body endure stress without breaking, supporting the nervous system when the urge to run feels overwhelming. It teaches the body that it can hold the intensity of anger without needing to flee from it.

And then there is Milky Oats. This is the ultimate nervous system trophorestorative—a true food for the nerves. When the fascia is tight and the nerves are raw, Milky Oats acts like a soothing balm. It is sweet, nourishing, and deeply restorative. Imagine it as a gentle hand resting on your clenched jaw, whispering to the masseter that it is safe to let go. You can find this support in a high-quality tincture or infusion, like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20), which can be taken daily to rebuild the frayed edges of your nervous system.

## The Wisdom of Resmaa Menakem

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist who focuses on the trauma held in the body, teaches that we cannot think our way out of these deeply ingrained patterns. The body, he explains, has its own intelligence, its own memory, and its own timeline for healing. When you run compulsively, you are not just dealing with a psychological issue; you are dealing with a somatic reality. The trauma of unexpressed anger is lodged in the tissues, in the psoas, in the diaphragm.

Menakem emphasizes the importance of "settling" the body. This doesn't mean forcing yourself to be calm, which often just creates more tension. Instead, it means noticing the activation—the tight jaw, the shallow breath, the urge to run—and meeting it with presence. It means allowing the body to feel the discomfort without immediately trying to fix it or flee from it.

When you feel the urge to lace up your shoes at 3 a.m., Menakem’s work invites you to pause. To notice the heat in your chest, the tightness in your trapezius. To ask the body, "What are you trying to outrun?" This is the beginning of somatic healing. It is the slow, sometimes agonizing work of staying with the body when every instinct screams at you to move.

## An Embodied Practice: Meeting the Urge to Run

The next time the compulsion to exercise hits you—when the restless energy surges through your legs and the psoas tightens in anticipation—try this practice. It is not designed to stop you from running, but to help you meet the urge with awareness.

Find a quiet place where you can stand or sit comfortably. Place one hand on your belly, just below your navel, and the other hand on your chest, over your heart. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or soften your gaze.

Notice the energy in your body. Where is it concentrated? Is it in the tightness of your jaw? The fluttering in your chest? The restless ache in your thighs? Don't try to change it; just observe it.

Now, bring your attention to your breath. You don't need to force it to be deep or slow. Just notice the rhythm. Is it shallow? Is it trapped high in your chest?

As you breathe, imagine that you are breathing directly into the areas of tension. If your psoas feels tight, imagine the breath traveling down into your pelvis, gently expanding the space around the muscle. If your jaw is clenched, imagine the breath softening the masseter.

Stay with this for a few minutes. If the urge to run becomes overwhelming, acknowledge it. Say to yourself, "I feel the urge to run. I feel the anger in my body." You are not fighting the compulsion; you are simply bringing it into the light of awareness.

When you are ready, slowly open your eyes. You may still choose to go for a run, but perhaps this time, you will run with a little more presence, a little more connection to the body that is carrying you.

## The Slow Work of Healing

Healing from compulsive exercise is not a linear journey. There will be days when the anger feels too big, when the urge to run is too strong, and you find yourself pounding the pavement once again. That is okay. The body’s patterns are deep, and they take time to unwind.

The work is in the returning. Returning to the breath, returning to the herbs, returning to the somatic practices that remind the body it is safe. It is in the slow, patient work of listening to the psoas, of softening the jaw, of honoring the adrenals.

You are not broken. Your body is not failing you. It is simply trying to survive the only way it knows how. By meeting that survival instinct with compassion, with the wisdom of teachers like Menakem and the support of herbal allies, you can begin to rewrite the story. You can learn to stay with the anger, to let it move through you, rather than constantly trying to outrun it.

## The Breath as an Anchor

When the urge to move becomes a frantic buzzing in your veins, your breath is the first thing to abandon you. It retreats high into the chest, shallow and rapid, mimicking the very panic your body is trying to escape. The diaphragm, that great umbrella of muscle beneath your lungs, becomes rigid. It forgets how to descend, how to massage the vagus nerve, how to signal to the nervous system that the threat has passed.

This is where the practice of embodied breathing becomes not just a tool, but a lifeline. It is the slow, deliberate act of reclaiming the diaphragm. When you force the breath down into the belly, you are physically pushing against the body’s panic response. You are telling the nervous system, "We are not running right now. We are staying."

It is not easy. The body will resist. The psoas will twitch, the levator scapulae will ache, and the mind will scream that you must move. But if you can hold the breath, if you can let it expand the ribcage and soften the belly, you begin to break the cycle. You begin to teach the body that stillness is not a death sentence, but a place of profound healing.

## The Medicine of Presence

The true medicine for compulsive exercise is not found in a pill or a perfect routine. It is found in presence. It is the willingness to be with the body exactly as it is, without trying to fix it, change it, or outrun it.

This presence is what Thich Nhat Hanh speaks of when he talks about mindfulness. It is the gentle, unwavering attention to the present moment. When you bring this presence to your body, you begin to unravel the knots of tension. You begin to hear the stories the fascia has been holding.

It is a radical act of self-love to simply sit with your anger, to feel the heat of it in your chest, the tightness of it in your jaw, and to say, "I am here. I am listening." This is the work of the somatic guide, the herbalist, the healer. It is the work of returning to the body, again and again, with infinite patience and compassion.

## The Final Surrender

There comes a moment in the healing journey when the exhaustion finally outweighs the compulsion. When the adrenals can no longer sustain the frantic pace, and the body simply demands rest. This is not a failure; it is a surrender.

It is the moment when you stop running and finally let the anger catch up to you. It will be terrifying. It will feel like a tidal wave threatening to pull you under. But if you can stay with it, if you can breathe through it, you will find that the anger is not a monster. It is just a feeling, a surge of energy that has been waiting for years to be acknowledged.

And when the wave passes, as all waves do, you will be left with a profound sense of peace. The psoas will soften, the jaw will relax, and the breath will return to its natural, deep rhythm. You will have stopped running, not because you gave up, but because you finally arrived.

## An Embodied Practice: The Surrender

Find a comfortable place to lie down. You can use a yoga mat, a bed, or even the floor. Place a pillow under your knees to support your lower back and allow the psoas to relax.

Close your eyes and take a deep breath in through your nose, filling your belly with air. Exhale slowly through your mouth, letting out a soft sigh. Repeat this three times.

Now, bring your attention to your body. Notice any areas of tension or holding. Don't try to change them; just observe them.

Imagine a warm, golden light starting at the top of your head and slowly moving down your body. As the light touches each part of your body, imagine the muscles softening and releasing.

Let the light wash over your forehead, your eyes, your jaw. Feel the masseter muscle relax, the teeth part slightly.

Let the light move down your neck, your shoulders, your arms. Feel the trapezius and levator scapulae melt into the surface beneath you.

Let the light flow into your chest, your belly, your pelvis. Feel the diaphragm expand and contract with ease. Feel the psoas release its grip on your lower back.

Let the light travel down your legs, your feet, your toes. Feel the restless energy drain away, replaced by a heavy, grounded sensation.

Stay in this state of deep relaxation for as long as you need. When you are ready to get up, do so slowly, rolling onto your side before pushing yourself up to a seated position. Carry this sense of surrender with you as you move through your day.

## The Body's Wisdom

Your body is not a machine to be fixed or a problem to be solved. It is a living, breathing ecosystem, full of wisdom and resilience. The anger you feel, the compulsion to run, the exhaustion of your adrenals—these are all part of the body's intricate language, its way of communicating with you.

When you learn to listen to this language, when you meet the body with compassion and presence, you begin to heal not just the physical symptoms, but the deep emotional wounds that lie beneath them. You begin to reclaim your body as a place of safety, a place of power, a place of profound wisdom.

And in doing so, you discover that you no longer need to outrun your anger. You can stand your ground, rooted in the earth, and let the fire burn without consuming you. You can be fierce, you can be angry, and you can be whole.

## The Integration of Herbs and Somatics

The journey of healing from compulsive exercise is a delicate dance between the physical and the emotional, the somatic and the herbal. It is about finding the right balance of support for your unique body and nervous system.

Herbs like Ashwagandha, Rhodiola, and Milky Oats provide the physical nourishment your body needs to repair the damage of chronic stress. They support the adrenals, soothe the nervous system, and rebuild the fascia. But they are most effective when combined with somatic practices that address the root cause of the tension.

When you take your herbs, do so with intention. Let it be a moment of connection with your body. As you swallow the tincture or sip the tea, imagine the medicine traveling to the areas of tension, bringing healing and release. Combine this with mindful breathing, with the practices of presence and surrender, and you create a powerful synergy that supports deep, lasting healing.

## The Path Forward

The path forward is not about never exercising again. It is about changing your relationship with movement. It is about moving from a place of joy, of connection, of celebration of the body's capabilities, rather than a place of fear, of avoidance, of compulsion.

It is about learning to listen to the body's signals—to rest when you are tired, to move when you have energy, to be still when you need to process emotion. It is about trusting the body's wisdom, knowing that it will guide you toward balance if you only give it the chance.

This is the work of a lifetime. It requires patience, compassion, and a willingness to be messy, to make mistakes, to start again. But it is the most important work you will ever do. Because in healing your relationship with your body, you heal your relationship with yourself, with your anger, with your power.

And that is a journey worth taking.

## The Final Word on Anger

Anger is not a moral failure. It is not a sign that you are broken or unspiritual. It is a vital, life-affirming energy, a messenger that tells you when your boundaries have been crossed, when your needs are not being met, when injustice has occurred.

When you stop running from your anger, when you turn and face it, you reclaim a piece of your soul. You reclaim the fire that fuels your passion, your creativity, your fierce love for yourself and the world.

So let the jaw soften. Let the psoas release. Let the breath deepen. And let the anger be what it is—a powerful, beautiful expression of your humanity.

## FAQ

**Q: How do I know if my exercise is compulsive or simply healthy movement?**  
**A:** Compulsive exercise is characterized by an urgent, relentless drive to move that overrides rest, pleasure, or bodily signals of fatigue. It often feels like a compulsion rather than a choice, accompanied by anxiety or guilt if not done. Healthy movement feels nourishing, balanced, and responsive to your body’s needs. If you find yourself waking before dawn, driven by a restless energy you can’t name, or pacing late at night to quell a fire inside, your body may be trying to tell you something beyond physical fitness.

**Q: I want to support my adrenals but am wary of supplements. How do I approach herbs safely?**  
**A:** Herbs like Ashwagandha, Rhodiola, and Milky Oats are powerful medicines and should be approached with respect. Start with small doses, ideally under the guidance of an herbalist or healthcare provider familiar with your unique history. Observe how your body responds, and remember that herbs support the slow work of nervous system regulation—they are not instant fixes. Quality matters, so seek reputable sources and avoid proprietary blends with unnecessary fillers.

**Q: What if I feel anger but don’t know how to express it safely?**  
**A:** You are not alone. Many women are raised to believe anger is unsafe, ugly, or wrong. Teachers like Robert Augustus Masters and Resmaa Menakem emphasize the importance of feeling anger as an embodied energy before finding ways to express it. Begin with somatic practices—tracking the body’s sensations, breathing into tightness, moving gently into the tension. Journaling, creative expression, or working with a somatic therapist can provide safe outlets. Remember, anger is a messenger, not a moral failing.

**Q: Can breathing really help with chronic tension and compulsive exercise?**  
**A:** Absolutely. Breath is the body’s direct line to the nervous system. When you are stuck in fight-or-flight, breath becomes shallow, rapid, and disconnected from the diaphragm. Mindful, deep breathing—especially into the belly and lower ribs—engages the parasympathetic nervous system, signaling safety and calming the adrenals. Teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh have long taught the power of “hugging the anger” through breath, meeting tension with presence rather than judgment.

**Q: What if I try to slow down but the restless energy in my legs and body won’t let me?**  
**A:** This is your body’s survival instinct, hardwired to keep you safe in a world that has not always allowed your anger to be seen. The twitching, the pacing, the restless legs are the psoas and nervous system’s way of trying to move energy that won’t yet settle. Gentle, compassionate somatic practices—like lying on your side with a hand on your belly, sipping calming herbs, or slow, mindful walking—can begin to teach your body that it’s safe to rest. It takes time and patience; the body’s memory is deep but not unchangeable.
